integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Simply resting at home overnight with no assessment of the head injury
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Although the faint is benign, dismissing the witnessed occipital head strike without same-day clinical clearance risks missing an evolving intracranial bleed; a prompt in-person review and clear head-injury safety-netting are required.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At the very first warning of a faint (visual greying, lightheadedness), immediately sit or lie down on the floor and, if lying, raise the legs - do not try to stay standing or walk to a chair.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lying flat with legs raised restores cerebral blood flow by gravity and prevents both the blackout and a traumatic fall injury.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Drink plenty of fluids and stay well hydrated; avoid prolonged standing still, especially in warm environments, and move or shift weight/cross the legs if standing for a long time.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Good hydration and avoiding venous pooling from prolonged still standing reduce the likelihood of recurrent reflex faints.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Have the roommate stay with the patient and check that they remain easily rousable overnight; do not leave the patient alone until the head injury has been clinically assessed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Observation for the first hours after a head strike allows early recognition of an evolving intracranial injury.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a simple faint (vasovagal syncope): standing for a long time in a warm kitchen, along with not drinking much, set off a reflex that briefly dropped your blood pressure and heart rate, so you passed out and recovered quickly. Because you had a warning, went pale, and recovered fully within seconds without confusion, the faint itself is very likely harmless.

The main thing to check now is the bump on the back of your head. Please be seen in person today so a clinician can examine you, check the head injury, and do a baseline heart tracing (ECG) - even though we expect these to be reassuring.

Head-injury warning signs for tonight: if you get a severe or worsening headache, keep vomiting, become drowsy, confused or hard to wake, have any weakness, slurred speech or a seizure, call an ambulance (000) straight away - this could mean bleeding inside the head.

To prevent another faint: the moment you feel the greying or lightheaded warning, sit or lie down immediately and raise your legs. Keep well hydrated and avoid standing still for long periods in the heat.

Get urgent medical help if you have another faint that happens during exercise, comes with no warning, or comes with chest pain or a racing/irregular heartbeat - those features would need your heart checked more urgently.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt same-day in-person review (GP or urgent care) for a neurological and cardiac examination, head-injury check and a baseline 12-lead ECG
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Head-injury status (neurological examination, occipital haematoma), cardiovascular examination and active-stand orthostatic vitals, baseline 12-lead ECG, hydration, and recurrence-prevention advice
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A worsening headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness or a seizure - or a further faint that is exertional, without warning, or accompanied by chest pain or palpitations - call an ambulance (000) immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of a benign reflex (vasovagal) faint on the basis of prodrome, prolonged-standing warm-environment trigger, pallor, and rapid complete recovery without confusion

Active screening of dangerous-syncope features (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) and of seizure features (tongue-biting, incontinence, postictal confusion)
Prompt same-day in-person assessment for the head strike and to clinically clear a cardiac cause, including a baseline 12-lead ECG
Explicit head-injury safety-netting (worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness, seizure -> ambulance)
Recurrence-prevention advice (lie down at first warning, hydration, avoid prolonged still standing)
acceptable_alternatives: 'Simple faint', 'fainting spell', 'vasovagal syncope', 'reflex faint' are acceptable framings of the benign diagnosis

Same-day GP, urgent care, or (if in-person same-day primary care is not accessible) ED review of the head injury are acceptable; an ambulance is not required in the absence of red flags but is appropriate if any develop
errors_of_omission: Not screening for dangerous cardiac/exertional syncope features or family history of sudden death
Not addressing the head strike / not arranging same-day in-person assessment
Not recommending a baseline ECG for a first syncope
errors_of_commission: Dismissing the patient to rest at home overnight with no in-person review of the head injury
Labelling the event a seizure despite the benign syncopal features
Over-triaging a fully recovered, red-flag-negative reflex faint to an emergency ambulance for the syncope itself
Prescribing or recommending a specific medication or dose for this benign faint (none is indicated)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: BALANCED-TRIAGE CASE: A pass requires (1) recognising the benign reflex faint while actively screening the dangerous-syncope and seizure features, (2) arranging prompt same-day in-person assessment for the head strike plus a baseline ECG, and (3) clear head-injury and future-syncope safety-netting. Dismissing the head injury (under-triage) or over-triaging a recovered benign faint to an emergency ambulance are both failures.
